skill.name: security-analyzer
skill.description: Security analysis skill for projects and infrastructure. It inventories dependencies, containers, IaC, and environment-file exposure, queries OSV.dev for dependency vulnerabilities, scores risk, and generates technical and executive remediation reports.
skill.summary: Inventory project assets, check OSV vulnerabilities, and produce remediation reports.
skill.author: Cornjebus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical heuristic because this skill combines scripts, filesystem discovery, network access, and security-related keywords. Manual review found no malicious intent or prompt injection, but confirmed moderate operational risk from OSV.dev queries, broad project enumeration, and generated remediation commands.

medium_findings.title: External OSV.dev Vulnerability Query
medium_findings.description: The scanner posts dependency package names, ecosystems, and versions to OSV.dev. This is legitimate vulnerability intelligence, but it can disclose private package metadata.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly sends package metadata to a fixed OSV.dev API endpoint. No secret values are sent, so the risk is metadata disclosure rather than exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Workspace File Enumeration and Environment File Discovery
medium_findings.description: The discovery script recursively scans a user-selected root and records dependency, container, IaC, and environment-file paths. It flags .env files but does not read or print secret values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file traversal and .env discovery are explicit. The surrounding code records paths and risk status, with no evidence that .env contents are logged or transmitted.

medium_findings.title: Generated Commands and Subprocess Test Snippets
medium_findings.description: The report generator emits package-manager commands and Python tests containing subprocess.run. The script does not execute them, but users should review generated commands before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static subprocess findings are inside generated report strings, not active execution. The residual risk is that generated remediation content may later be copied and run.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command alerts point to triple-backtick Markdown examples in the skill instructions and report template. These are documentation blocks, not Ruby or shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fences or inline examples. They are not executable code paths in the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Keyword Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and weak-crypto alerts match ordinary text near ecosystem mapping and security documentation. The .env handling finds file paths and explicitly says not to log values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no SAM database access and no weak cryptographic implementation. Environment-file logic records names and exposure state, not secret contents.

low_findings.title: Hard Link and Path Traversal Alerts Are Token-Level False Positives
low_findings.description: Hard-link and path-traversal alerts map to loop variables, grouping logic, and report formatting. No evidence found of os.link, symlink abuse, or path traversal writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not create links or normalize attacker-controlled paths. The scanner appears to have matched substrings inside unrelated words or templates.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP POST to External Vulnerability API
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill sends dependency metadata to https://api.osv.dev/v1/query for vulnerability lookup. This is expected behavior but should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and POST request are explicit. The context confirms intended vulnerability lookup rather than hidden data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Project File Discovery
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recursively searches the chosen root for dependency manifests, container files, IaC files, and .env filenames.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The traversal is a core feature and is visible in the discovery flow. It is bounded by the user-provided root and skips common large folders.

dangerous_patterns.title: Report-Generated Subprocess Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The report generator writes validation test examples containing subprocess.run. These examples are not executed by the generator.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls appear inside formatted strings returned as report content. There is no direct subprocess execution in the generator itself.

content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss dependency, container, and IaC risks spread across a repository. This skill inventories assets, checks OSV data, and creates prioritized remediation reports.
actual_capabilities: Discovers npm, pip, gem, Go, Cargo, Maven, and NuGet dependency manifests.
Finds Docker, Docker Compose, Kubernetes, Terraform, CloudFormation, and Bicep configuration files.
Flags environment files that may be exposed by missing .gitignore rules.
Queries OSV.dev for known dependency vulnerabilities by package and version.
Calculates vulnerability risk scores using severity, exploitability, criticality, and exposure factors.
Generates technical and executive remediation reports with validation test examples.
limitations: It sends dependency names and versions to OSV.dev during vulnerability lookup.
It does not automatically verify CISA KEV, NVD, or GitHub Advisory results.
Generated fix commands and tests need review before use in production.
It is not a replacement for manual penetration testing or compliance review.

use_cases.title: Pre-release security review
use_cases.description: Scan a repository before release and prioritize dependency fixes by risk.

use_cases.title: Infrastructure posture check
use_cases.description: Inventory container and IaC files before a platform hardening sprint.

use_cases.title: Leadership risk summary
use_cases.description: Create an executive summary that explains vulnerability counts, effort, and remediation phases.

prompt_templates.title: Basic repository scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner scan of the current project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a security scan on this repository and summarize the highest-risk findings first.

prompt_templates.title: Dependency vulnerability review
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this project for vulnerable dependencies, then list affected packages, severity, and safe upgrade paths.

prompt_templates.title: Container and IaC assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Review container, Kubernetes, Terraform, and CloudFormation files for security issues and remediation priorities.

prompt_templates.title: Remediation plan with validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a phased remediation plan with validation tests, estimated effort, and separate technical and executive summaries.

output_examples.input: Run a dependency security scan before release.
output_examples.output: A prioritized vulnerability report with package names, severity levels, risk scores, and recommended upgrade actions.

output_examples.input: Assess our Docker and Terraform files.
output_examples.output: An inventory of infrastructure files, likely exposure points, and remediation tasks grouped by urgency.
An executive summary with total findings, top risks, estimated engineering effort, and near-term actions.

best_practices: Run the skill on a scoped project directory, not an entire home directory.
Review dependency metadata disclosure before querying OSV.dev for private packages.
Validate generated fixes in a branch with tests and code review.
anti_patterns: Do not paste secrets or environment file contents into prompts or reports.
Do not run generated package-manager commands without reviewing version impact.
Do not treat the report as a complete penetration test or compliance attestation.

faq.question: Does this skill read secret values from .env files?
faq.answer: No evidence found that it reads secret values. It records environment-file paths and exposure status.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It queries OSV.dev with package names, ecosystems, and versions to find vulnerabilities.

faq.question: Can it scan private dependencies?
faq.answer: It can list private package names if they appear in manifests. Review metadata disclosure before scanning.

faq.question: Does it execute remediation commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It generates commands and test examples for review by engineers.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill has legitimate security functions, but it uses scripts, filesystem scanning, network queries, and generated commands.
